title: Apps | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Daily apps and exclusions for the mac.riera.co.uk rebuild.
body: The app list is small enough to remember and explicit enough not to drift. Current App Groups Dev Browsers + AI Productivity Backup Capture Network Replacements And Watchlist KeeWeb → KeePassXC MailMate beta Kap Explicit Exclusions New Additions Tailscale OrbStack BetterDisplay Little Snitch Apps | mac.riera.co.uk Apps The app list is small enough to remember and explicit enough not to drift. Publishing the app roster matters because the repo doubles as documentation. The fastest way to keep it honest is to be explicit about both the tools that stay and the ones that are gone. Keyboard Maestro handles the launcher and clipboard role. Rectangle Pro handles window management. BetterDisplay, Obsidian, Logseq, MailMate beta, Shottr, Kap, and Vivaldi stay in the everyday path. Current App Groups These match the generated Homebrew groupings in the repo. Dev OrbStack sits next to Go, Pandoc, Jira CLI, and Volta because local container work belongs in the rebuild. Browsers + AI Vivaldi, DuckDuckGo, VS Code, Codex, Claude, Antigravity. Productivity Keyboard Maestro, Rectangle Pro, Karabiner-Elements, BetterDisplay, Obsidian, Logseq, MailMate beta, KeePassXC, Spotify. Backup Time Machine to TrueNAS is the default answer. Kopia stays optional if a second backup layer is still needed. Capture Shottr, Kap, and ffmpeg. Network Tailscale, Little Snitch, RustDesk, NetSpot, and the CLI diagnostics that only need to appear on heavier rebuilds. Replacements And Watchlist The public list stays useful only if stale tools are called out explicitly. KeeWeb → KeePassXC KeeWeb stayed valid in Homebrew but its desktop releases have stalled. KeePassXC is the chosen replacement. MailMate beta The repo now chooses mailmate@beta on Apple Silicon so MailMate stays native and avoids Rosetta 2. Kap Kap stays. It still has a current Homebrew cask and remains a clean lightweight recorder. Explicit Exclusions Keeping the “no” list visible is part of keeping the rebuild stable. App Expose is off. Edge is out. Vivaldi is the browser path. Alfred and Raycast are out. Keyboard Maestro covers that ground. pnpm is out. Volta stays in. Dropover, iBar, Whimsical, and Notion are out of the default rebuild. New Additions These tools are now part of the tracked setup instead of sitting on the sidelines. Tailscale Added in the network slice so NFS-backed machines, support boxes, and private services stay reachable without opening ports. OrbStack Added in the dev slice as the default local container and Linux path instead of Docker Desktop. BetterDisplay Added in productivity because external monitor scaling, DDC brightness control, and awkward display layouts keep coming up. Little Snitch Added in the network slice for tighter visibility into outbound traffic on a Mac that also does network and support work.

title: Audit | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Current package audit for the mac.riera.co.uk setup.
body: Package audit snapshot for March 12, 2026. What Checked Out Core CLI formulae Daily casks Jira CLI Backup tooling Replacement Chosen Removed from default list New default Watchlist, Not Removal Audit | mac.riera.co.uk Audit Package audit snapshot for March 12, 2026. The Homebrew names in the repo were re-checked against the current catalog and the higher-risk projects were spot-checked upstream for release activity. The package list is still valid, but one replacement was worth making. Chosen replacement: KeeWeb → KeePassXC . KeeWeb is still installable, but its desktop releases have stalled long enough that it no longer belongs in the default rebuild. What Checked Out The core list remains viable. These are the packages that did not show abandonment or naming issues during the audit. Core CLI formulae bash, wget, vim, uv, tig, htop, tree, tmux, jq, go, pandoc, volta, ffmpeg, rclone, mtr, nmap, and the network tools are still current Homebrew entries. Daily casks Vivaldi, DuckDuckGo, VS Code, Codex, Claude, Keyboard Maestro, Rectangle Pro, BetterDisplay, Obsidian, Logseq, MailMate beta, KeePassXC, Shottr, RustDesk, NetSpot, Tailscale App, Little Snitch, Antigravity, Kap, and OrbStack all still have current Homebrew entries. Jira CLI jira-cli is still active in Homebrew and its upstream project remains current, so it stays in the dev bundle. Backup tooling Kopia and KopiaUI are still current Homebrew entries, but they now sit as optional extras rather than the default backup path. Replacement Chosen The replacement is already reflected in the install data and generated Brewfiles. Removed from default list KeeWeb remains installable, but its latest desktop release trail is old enough that it now sits in “works, but stale” territory. New default KeePassXC is the replacement because it is actively released, broadly used, and a better long-term fit for a KeePass-compatible desktop workflow. Watchlist, Not Removal These tools stay, but with a note attached. MailMate stays, but the repo now chooses mailmate@beta on Apple Silicon because the stable Homebrew cask currently requires Rosetta 2. Kap stays because it still has a current cask and a clear niche in the workflow, even though it is not the only capture option anymore. Logseq stays as a secondary knowledge tool next to Obsidian, with git-friendly file graphs making it easy to back up privately.

title: Backup | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Backup tooling and backup layout for the mac.riera.co.uk rebuild.
body: Time Machine to TrueNAS is good enough if it already covers the job. Recommended Backup Shape Time Machine Checks Latest backup Backup destination Optional Kopia extras What To Protect Automation Knowledge Mail and identity Exclude the noise Keyboard Maestro And GitHub Keep live sync simple Use git for snapshots Do the same for Logseq Backup | mac.riera.co.uk Backup Time Machine to TrueNAS is good enough if it already covers the job. If Time Machine to the TrueNAS or NFS target gives a full backup and a restore path you trust, there is no need to add Kopia just for the sake of it. Keep backup simple unless a real gap shows up. Default answer: use Time Machine . Treat Kopia as optional only when you want an extra encrypted offsite copy, repository verification, or a second backup shape beyond Time Machine. If the goal is a full wipe, use as the companion page. Time Machine is the full-machine safety net before Erase Assistant. Recommended Backup Shape Start with the smaller plan first. Add more only if Time Machine is not covering something important. Use Time Machine with the TrueNAS or NFS destination and confirm backups complete normally. Before a reset or major upgrade, confirm the latest Time Machine snapshot exists and can be browsed. Keep app exports and small irreplaceable files in a separate private path if you want faster selective restore. Add Kopia only if you still need encrypted offsite storage or repository-style verification beyond Time Machine. Time Machine Checks These are the commands worth keeping close if Time Machine is the main backup path. Latest backup Confirm the most recent Time Machine snapshot exists. Backup destination Confirm which destination Time Machine is using. Optional Kopia extras Only relevant if Time Machine is not enough and you want the extra layer. What To Protect These are the paths that matter more than generic app binaries, because Homebrew can reinstall apps but it cannot recreate state. Automation ~/Library/Application Support/Keyboard Maestro , exported macro folders, and any text expander source files. Knowledge Obsidian vaults, Logseq graphs, plugin settings, and any repo-backed knowledge projects. Mail and identity MailMate settings, dotfiles, SSH material, repo clones, and the small app preference directories that would be annoying to rebuild. Exclude the noise Skip caches, node_modules, derived build output, downloaded dependencies, and media you can already re-fetch. Keyboard Maestro And GitHub GitHub is useful here, but not as a live sync replacement. Keep live sync simple Continue using Google Drive for live Keyboard Maestro sync if it is already stable. That is the low-risk path. Use git for snapshots Export all macros as a folder and store those exports in a private Git repo for version history and rollback. Do the same for Logseq Logseq file graphs are a good fit for a private Git repo, which also gives you change history across notes and config.

title: Browser | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Browser defaults for the mac.riera.co.uk setup.
body: Vivaldi first, DuckDuckGo by default. What To Set Immediately Default browser Default search Profile restore Noise control Browser Checklist Browser | mac.riera.co.uk Browser Vivaldi first, DuckDuckGo by default. The browser setup is simple on purpose. No Edge, no Chrome-first posture, and no wandering search defaults. Vivaldi is the main browser and DuckDuckGo is the default search engine inside it. The browsers-ai Homebrew slice installs Vivaldi, DuckDuckGo, VS Code, Codex, Claude, and Antigravity together. What To Set Immediately These are the browser checks worth doing before other app state starts piling on. Default browser Set Vivaldi as the macOS default browser so external links stop opening in whatever macOS guessed first. Default search Set DuckDuckGo as the default search engine inside Vivaldi, not just in the companion browser app. Profile restore Sign into the main Vivaldi profile and let sync pull tabs, workspaces, bookmarks, and extensions back in. Noise control Skip the browsers and launchers that are no longer part of the daily stack. Browser Checklist A tight browser checklist is better than a long browser essay. Install Vivaldi and DuckDuckGo via Homebrew. Set Vivaldi as the default browser in macOS. Set DuckDuckGo as the default search engine in Vivaldi. Restore the Vivaldi profile and confirm workspaces, bookmarks, and extensions are back. Pin the core sites and log back into the coding and note-taking tabs that matter daily.

title: Coding | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Coding setup for the mac.riera.co.uk rebuild.
body: VS Code, Codex, Claude, Antigravity, OrbStack, and just enough runtime tooling. Editor Restore VS Code Codex Claude Antigravity OrbStack Volta Reference Screens VS Code Look code Command Coding | mac.riera.co.uk Coding VS Code, Codex, Claude, Antigravity, OrbStack, and just enough runtime tooling. The development setup is intentionally narrow: install the editor, install the assistants, restore sync, keep Node.js versioning on Volta, and use OrbStack for the local container path. No pnpm detour, no extra package-manager layer. The dev slice keeps Go, Pandoc, Jira CLI, Volta, and OrbStack together because they belong on a real workstation. Editor Restore Install the tools, sign in, and pull the environment back into shape quickly. VS Code Install from Homebrew, sign in, and let Settings Sync pull extensions, settings, and UI state back. Codex Install the current Codex app and reconnect the workflow you use for repo work. Claude Install the desktop app and restore the project-level workflow you use next to VS Code and Codex. Antigravity Install the current Google Antigravity app if it stays part of the coding loop, especially when you want a second agent workflow outside VS Code. OrbStack Keep local containers and Linux workloads on OrbStack so the machine can run Docker-compatible workflows without falling back to Docker Desktop. Volta Keep Node.js version management in Volta so project runtimes stay clean without pulling pnpm into the base install. Reference Screens Visual cues matter when rebuilding fast and verifying the terminal integration is back. VS Code Look Sanity-check fonts, editor chrome, and overall feel after sync lands. code Command Confirm the VS Code command-line entry point is available from the shell.

title: Factory Reset | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: How to back up a MacBook Pro and reset it to factory settings before starting over.
body: Back up the whole machine, then erase it cleanly. Pre-Wipe Backup Stack 1. Time Machine first 2. Optional selective exports 3. Export the awkward bits 4. Sync the cloud tools Time Machine Checklist Verify latest Time Machine backup Factory Reset On Apple Silicon If Erase Assistant Is Unavailable Older Intel-only route Stay connected After The Wipe Factory Reset | mac.riera.co.uk Factory Reset Back up the whole machine, then erase it cleanly. This is the path to use before selling a Mac, handing it to someone else, or deliberately starting over. The backup here is full-machine first, selective exports second. The reset path follows current Apple-silicon guidance. Short version: run a fresh Time Machine backup, verify it, then use Erase Assistant from System Settings. Pre-Wipe Backup Stack Use a full-system backup plus the app-state backups that matter to this setup. 1. Time Machine first Use Time Machine as the safety net for the entire Mac. That is the backup Apple points to before erasing a machine. 2. Optional selective exports Export the few awkward bits you would rather restore selectively. This does not require Kopia if Time Machine already covers the machine. 3. Export the awkward bits Export Keyboard Maestro macros, keep Karabiner and Little Snitch exports close, and make sure Obsidian, Logseq, and MailMate state is where you expect it. 4. Sync the cloud tools Confirm Vivaldi, VS Code, and any other account-backed tools have finished syncing before the wipe starts. Time Machine Checklist This is the fastest whole-machine backup path before a reset. Connect an external backup disk or mount the Time Machine network destination. Open Time Machine settings and add the backup disk if it is not already configured. Start a backup and wait for it to complete before you erase the Mac. Run tmutil latestbackup in Terminal to confirm the most recent backup exists. If the wipe is precautionary rather than urgent, spot-check that the latest backup contains the files you care about most. Verify latest Time Machine backup A quick CLI check before Erase Assistant. Factory Reset On Apple Silicon This is the normal path for a current MacBook Pro. Open System Settings . Go to General → Transfer or Reset . Choose Erase All Content and Settings . Enter the administrator password and review what will be removed. Continue through Erase Assistant and let the Mac reboot to the Hello screen. Apple says Erase Assistant signs you out of Apple services, turns off Find My and Activation Lock, removes apps you installed, erases all volumes, and deletes all user accounts and their data. If Erase Assistant Is Unavailable This is the fallback path, not the normal one for Apple-silicon MacBook Pros. Older Intel-only route Apple documents a separate Recovery-based erase-and-reinstall flow for Intel Macs without the T2 chip. Keep it as a reference, but do not reach for it first on modern MacBook Pros. Stay connected If you do end up in Recovery, Apple calls out two basics: keep the Mac online and keep a laptop on power while reinstalling macOS. After The Wipe Choose the restore path based on whether you want a clean rebuild or a fast migration. If the goal is a clean rebuild, set the Mac up as new and follow this site from the beginning. If the goal is a faster return, use Migration Assistant to pull accounts, apps, files, and settings from a Time Machine backup. Keep any app exports you made available even if you migrate, because selective restore is often cleaner than moving everything back.

title: mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Opinionated MacBook Pro rebuild guide for Joan Marc Riera.
body: Mac setup, without the drift. Future-Self Flow What This Site Optimizes For Copy-paste installs Minimal zsh Keyboard-first workflow Public and maintainable Page Map mac.riera.co.uk Overview Mac setup, without the drift. This repo is the public version of a personal rebuild guide. It keeps the stack honest: Vivaldi, DuckDuckGo, zsh, Keyboard Maestro, VS Code, Codex, Claude, Antigravity, OrbStack, BetterDisplay, Obsidian, Logseq, MailMate beta, Shottr, Kap, Rectangle Pro, and the backup flow that starts with Time Machine to TrueNAS. 0 Homebrew formulae tracked in the repo 0 apps wired into the install data 0 presets exposed in the installer Future-Self Flow The quickest rebuild path is short on purpose. Copy the commands you need, then finish the few settings that still require clicks. Install Homebrew and copy the Minimal , Workstation , or Full command from the Homebrew page. Copy the macOS defaults commands you want from the macOS Defaults page. Restore app state for Vivaldi, Keyboard Maestro, Rectangle Pro, VS Code, Obsidian, and Karabiner. Run ./scripts/verify-setup.sh workstation and finish the remaining manual checks. What This Site Optimizes For It is not a generic “best Mac apps” list. It is a crisp rebuild guide for one specific working setup. Copy-paste installs Preset and group commands are generated from the same data source as the repo Brewfiles. Minimal zsh Only the useful parts stay. Legacy plugin habits do not get carried forward. Keyboard-first workflow Keyboard Maestro takes the launcher and clipboard role. Alfred and Raycast stay out. Public and maintainable The Pages site, scripts, and generated artifacts can be validated in CI before publishing. Page Map Use the site as either a linear checklist or a reference library. Preset and group command tables with raw brew install lines. Trackpad, Dock, Launchpad, and other preferences listed as commands or short manual paths. Vivaldi as the default browser and DuckDuckGo as the default search engine. Dark zsh setup, minimal plugins, and no legacy shell extras. VS Code, Codex, Claude, Antigravity, OrbStack, Volta, and the core development restore checklist. The current app roster plus the exclusions that keep the stack from drifting again. Time Machine first, with Kopia kept optional only if the TrueNAS path is not enough. Full-machine backup plus Apple-silicon reset steps before a clean rebuild, resale, or handoff. Validation notes for the current package list, including replacements for stale projects. The manual bits that matter after installation: sync, licenses, shortcuts, and profile imports. Scripts and checks to confirm the machine actually matches the documented setup. Site-wide search for pages, groups, bundles, and individual Homebrew package names.

title: macOS Defaults | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Manual macOS defaults and shell commands for the mac.riera.co.uk setup.
body: Copy the macOS setting you want and apply it directly. Preference List Tap to Click Three-Finger Drag Dock Autohide Speed Launchpad 10x8 Restore Dock and Launchpad App Expose Spotlight Shortcut Reference Screens Tap to Click Three-Finger Drag Launchpad 10x8 Spotlight Handoff macOS Defaults | mac.riera.co.uk macOS Defaults Copy the macOS setting you want and apply it directly. No wrapper script needed here. Each common preference is listed with either a direct command or the exact System Settings path. This setup keeps Tap to Click and three-finger drag , speeds up Dock autohide, expands Launchpad to 10x8, and keeps App Expose turned off. Preference List Use the command when one exists. Otherwise follow the short manual path. Tap to Click Trackpad click on touch. Three-Finger Drag Still easiest through the UI. Dock Autohide Speed Remove the Dock delay and make the animation faster. Launchpad 10x8 Set the denser Launchpad grid. Restore Dock and Launchpad Back out the Dock timing and Launchpad grid tweaks. App Expose Leave it off. Spotlight Shortcut Free the launcher shortcut for Keyboard Maestro if needed. Reference Screens Keeping the images nearby makes the manual path faster when the UI changes names slightly. Tap to Click Trackpad → Point & Click Three-Finger Drag Accessibility → Pointer Control → Trackpad Options Launchpad 10x8 Reference view after the Dock restart. Spotlight Handoff Disable Spotlight if Keyboard Maestro is taking the launcher shortcut.

title: Post-Install | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Post-install verification for the mac.riera.co.uk rebuild.
body: Verify the rebuild before calling it done. Commands Rebuild generated files Rebuild search index Verify workstation Manual Final Pass Local Preview and CI Preview locally Validate docs Validate generated files Validate search index Post-Install | mac.riera.co.uk Post-Install Verify the rebuild before calling it done. The repo now includes scripts and CI so the public site and the working setup can be checked instead of trusted. Run the scripts locally, then use the remaining manual checks as a final pass. Verification order: generate artifacts if data changed, apply defaults, run the setup check, then finish the browser and Keyboard Maestro handoff. Commands These are the repo-level commands worth keeping close after a rebuild. Rebuild generated files Run after editing install data or package groups. Rebuild search index Run after editing the site pages. Verify workstation Checks Homebrew installs and Dock/Launchpad defaults. Manual Final Pass The scripts cover the repeatable parts. These are the checks still worth doing by eye. Tap to Click and three-finger drag are both working. Vivaldi opens as the default browser and uses DuckDuckGo by default. Keyboard Maestro shortcuts are firing correctly for launcher and clipboard history. VS Code sync has restored the expected extensions and editor feel. Codex, Claude, Antigravity, and OrbStack are signed in and usable. MailMate beta launches natively, accounts connect cleanly, and Logseq graphs open where expected. BetterDisplay profiles apply correctly and Tailscale plus Little Snitch behave the way this machine expects. Shottr, Kap, and Rectangle Pro all respond with the expected shortcuts. If Time Machine is the backup path, confirm the TrueNAS destination is mounted and tmutil latestbackup returns the expected snapshot. Local Preview and CI The Pages site is static. It is easy to preview and validate before publishing. Preview locally python3 -m http.server --directory docs 8000 gives a quick local preview of the Pages site. Validate docs python3 scripts/check_docs.py checks internal file references and required Pages files. Validate generated files python3 scripts/generate_brew_artifacts.py --check catches drift between the data source and generated outputs. Validate search index python3 scripts/build_search_index.py --check catches drift between the public pages and the client-side search index.

title: Restore State | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Manual restore checklist for the mac.riera.co.uk rebuild.
body: Installation is fast. State restore is where the machine becomes yours again. Core Restore Checklist Things Worth Storing Outside This Repo Licenses Exports Account-heavy setups Restore State | mac.riera.co.uk Restore State Installation is fast. State restore is where the machine becomes yours again. Homebrew gets the apps on disk. This page is the short manual list for the things that still have to be signed into, licensed, or nudged back into the right shape. Keep this page close while rebuilding. It is the part most likely to be forgotten after a few months away. Core Restore Checklist Do these in roughly this order so the machine starts feeling normal quickly. Sign into Vivaldi and confirm sync brings back workspaces, bookmarks, and extensions. Sign into VS Code and enable Settings Sync. Reconnect Codex and Claude to the repos and workflows you use daily. Restore Antigravity if it is part of the current coding flow. Import or sync Keyboard Maestro macros and reconnect launcher and clipboard shortcuts. Activate Rectangle Pro and restore the expected window movement shortcuts. Reconnect Karabiner-Elements rules if the keyboard layer is part of the current machine. Restore BetterDisplay presets if external monitor layouts and brightness mappings matter on this desk. Open Obsidian and reconnect vaults and plugins. Reconnect Logseq graphs and confirm any git-based graph backup still points at the right remote. Restore MailMate accounts, IMAP settings, and message rules. Sign into Tailscale, restore Little Snitch rules if you export them, and confirm OrbStack starts with the expected local container state. Check Shottr and Kap preferences if output folders or shortcut bindings matter. Things Worth Storing Outside This Repo Document the restore source, not the secret itself. Licenses Keyboard Maestro and Rectangle Pro license material should live in a secure password manager, not in git. Exports Keyboard Maestro exports, browser backup flows, Logseq graphs, Karabiner profiles, and any Little Snitch rule exports should live in a separate private backup path. Account-heavy setups Keep a note of which logins must be restored, but avoid turning the repo into a secrets checklist.

title: Terminal | mac.riera.co.uk
summary: Terminal and zsh defaults for the mac.riera.co.uk setup.
body: Dark terminal, 20% transparency, plain zsh. Shell Baseline Minimal plugin list What not to reintroduce Terminal Look Transparency Reference Natural Text Editing Terminal | mac.riera.co.uk Terminal Dark terminal, 20% transparency, plain zsh. The terminal setup should feel familiar within minutes, not after a theme digression. The shell stays on zsh, the look stays dark with about 20% transparency, and the plugin list stays short. Legacy shell extras stay out. autojump and zsh-syntax-highlighting are intentionally not part of this rebuild. Shell Baseline The goal is a clean, portable shell profile that does not need constant babysitting. Minimal plugin list Only keep what is still worth it. What not to reintroduce No autojump. No zsh-syntax-highlighting. No pnpm in the shell bootstrap path. Terminal Look Keep it visually familiar enough that the machine feels like yours immediately. Transparency Reference Use a dark profile with about 20% transparency. Natural Text Editing If the terminal supports it, keep familiar key bindings for word-wise editing.

title: Productivity
summary: Window management, keyboard automation, notes, and personal utilities.
body: keyboard-maestro rectangle-pro karabiner-elements betterdisplay obsidian logseq mailmate@beta keepassxc spotify Keyboard Maestro replaces Alfred, Raycast, and standalone clipboard managers. Keyboard Maestro and Rectangle Pro still require paid licenses after install. BetterDisplay stays because external monitor scaling and brightness control keep coming up. MailMate beta is the default on Apple Silicon because the stable cask currently requires Rosetta 2. KeeWeb was intentionally replaced with KeePassXC because KeeWeb's desktop releases have stalled.

title: Backup
summary: Optional backup extras if Time Machine to TrueNAS is not enough.
body: kopia kopiaui google-drive If Time Machine to the TrueNAS or NFS target already gives a complete backup and clean restore path, skip this group. Kopia stays optional for cases where an extra encrypted offsite copy or repository-style verification is still useful.

title: kopia
body: Optional backup extras if Time Machine to TrueNAS is not enough. If Time Machine to the TrueNAS or NFS target already gives a complete backup and clean restore path, skip this group. Kopia stays optional for cases where an extra encrypted offsite copy or repository-style verification is still useful.

title: Network
summary: Diagnostics, remote access, and support tooling kept out of the default rebuild.
body: telnet rclone ipmitool openssl lftp mtr nmap net-snmp tailscale-app little-snitch rustdesk netspot Useful when the Mac is doubling as a support or network box. The current Homebrew cask token is tailscale-app, not tailscale.
